question: What is the key rule for a Domain Store?

options.text: It operates in domain terms and knows nothing about the UI
It contains the state of filters, modals, and tabs
options.explanation: Filters, modals, and tabs are UI state that belongs to UI Store, not Domain Store.

options.text: It must be the only store in the application
options.explanation: An application can have many Domain Stores (TodoStore, UserStore, OrderStore) — one for each domain area.

options.text: It should directly import other stores
options.explanation: Direct imports between stores create circular dependencies. Root Store is used to connect stores.

question: What problem does Root Store solve?

options.text: It eliminates circular dependencies between stores by acting as a mediator
It speeds up MobX by centralizing observables
options.explanation: Root Store doesn't affect MobX performance. Its purpose is organizing connections between stores without direct imports.

options.text: It automatically wraps all child stores in observer
options.explanation: Root Store is not related to observer. observer is a HOC for React components, while Root Store is a store organization pattern.

options.text: It replaces React Context for passing data
options.explanation: Root Store is typically passed via React Context. It doesn't replace Context but complements it as a single access point to stores.

question: When should you use model classes instead of plain objects in a store's array?

options.text: When the entity has its own behavior (methods)
When computed properties are needed at the individual item level
When the entity is complex with nested data
Always — plain objects can't be used in MobX
options.explanation: MobX works perfectly with plain objects. Models are needed when entities have behavior or computed properties. If the object is just data, plain objects suffice.

question: Why shouldn't you store derived data as observable instead of computed?

options.text: Observable needs manual updates in every action, which risks desynchronization
MobX doesn't allow storing numbers and strings as observable
options.explanation: MobX can store any type as observable. The issue isn't about types but about data duplication and manual synchronization.

options.text: Computed values are faster than observable in terms of performance
options.explanation: Performance isn't the main point here. The key issue is reliability: computed automatically derives from sources, while a manually updated observable can get out of sync.

options.text: TypeScript doesn't allow having an observable property and computed with the same name
options.explanation: This isn't a TypeScript limitation. The problem is architectural: a manually updated observable property duplicates data and can become stale.

question: What happens if you forget to call makeAutoObservable in a model class (e.g., Todo)?

options.text: Model properties won't be tracked by MobX, and observer components won't react to their changes
The application will crash with a runtime error
options.explanation: The app won't crash — the code will work, but changes to model properties (e.g., todo.completed) won't trigger re-renders in observer components.

options.text: TypeScript will show a compilation error
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't know about MobX reactivity and won't show an error. The problem manifests only at runtime — components won't update.

options.text: The collection store won't be able to add the model instance to the array
options.explanation: JavaScript allows adding any object to an array. Without makeAutoObservable, the model simply won't be reactive.
